one_liner: A 38-year-old woman presents with 9 months of progressive weight gain centered on her face and trunk, new purple stretch marks on her abdomen, easy bruising, and recently diagnosed hypertension and hyperglycemia.

stage2.hpi_extension: Her weight has increased 25 pounds despite no change in diet. She has developed a round red face, a prominent posterior cervical fat pad, and wide purple abdominal striae. She notes proximal muscle weakness — she struggles to rise from a chair and climb stairs — and bruises easily. Her menses have become irregular. She denies exogenous steroid use. She has had increased acne and fine facial hair.
stage2.pmh: New-onset hypertension 4 months ago; new-onset hyperglycemia
stage2.physical_exam: General: **central (truncal) obesity** with thin extremities. HEENT: **rounded 'moon' facies** with facial plethora. Neck: **dorsocervical fat pad ('buffalo hump')** and supraclavicular fat pads. CV: hypertensive, regular rhythm. Abdomen: obese with **wide purple abdominal striae (>1 cm)**. Skin: **thin, easily bruised**; mild hirsutism; acne. MSK: **proximal muscle weakness** (difficulty rising from chair). Neuro: intact.

lab_results.result: **24-hour urine free cortisol markedly elevated** (4x upper limit). **Late-night salivary cortisol elevated**. **Low-dose (1 mg overnight) dexamethasone suppression test: FAILURE to suppress** morning cortisol below 1.8 µg/dL. Subsequent **high-dose dexamethasone: partial suppression** (consistent with pituitary source).
lab_results.clinical_significance: Three positive screening tests confirm endogenous hypercortisolism (Cushing syndrome). Failure of low-dose dexamethasone to suppress cortisol excludes normal HPA axis feedback. Partial suppression with high-dose dexamethasone suggests Cushing disease (pituitary ACTH-producing adenoma) rather than ectopic ACTH or adrenal source.

lab_results.result: **ACTH elevated** (inappropriately, for the high cortisol). Morning cortisol high. DHEA-S mildly elevated.
lab_results.clinical_significance: ACTH-dependent hypercortisolism. Elevated (rather than suppressed) ACTH rules out adrenal adenoma/carcinoma. Differential is pituitary Cushing disease vs. ectopic ACTH secretion (classically small cell lung cancer).

Cortisol excess drives gluconeogenesis (hyperglycemia), and at very high levels cortisol saturates 11-beta-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase type 2 and acts on renal mineralocorticoid receptors, producing hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis and hypertension.

stage5.final_clue: Pituitary MRI reveals a 7 mm pituitary microadenoma. She undergoes transsphenoidal resection, and her cortisol and clinical stigmata improve over months.

debrief.classic_presentation: Cushing syndrome = clinical manifestations of chronic glucocorticoid excess. Classic findings include central obesity, moon facies, buffalo hump, supraclavicular fat pads, purple abdominal striae >1 cm, thin skin, easy bruising, proximal muscle weakness, hypertension, glucose intolerance, osteoporosis, amenorrhea, hirsutism, acne, and psychiatric changes. The most common overall cause is iatrogenic glucocorticoid therapy; the most common endogenous cause is Cushing disease (pituitary ACTH adenoma).
debrief.pathophysiology: Chronic cortisol excess causes catabolic effects (muscle wasting, thin skin, striae, osteoporosis), insulin antagonism (hyperglycemia), protein breakdown, and fat redistribution to the face/trunk/dorsocervical area. At supraphysiologic levels cortisol spills over to the mineralocorticoid receptor, causing Na+ retention, hypertension, and hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis. Causes: (1) exogenous steroids (most common); (2) Cushing disease — pituitary ACTH adenoma (most common endogenous); (3) ectopic ACTH (small cell lung cancer, bronchial carcinoid); (4) adrenal adenoma/carcinoma.
debrief.treatment_overview: Directed at underlying cause: transsphenoidal pituitary adenoma resection for Cushing disease, adrenalectomy for adrenal tumors, resection of ectopic ACTH source (e.g., lung tumor). Medical therapy (ketoconazole, metyrapone, mifepristone, pasireotide) can lower cortisol pre-op or when surgery is not feasible. Iatrogenic cases require slow steroid taper to avoid adrenal crisis. First Aid workup: screen with 24-hour urine free cortisol, late-night salivary cortisol, and low-dose dexamethasone suppression; localize with ACTH, high-dose dexamethasone suppression, and imaging (pituitary MRI, adrenal CT, chest CT).
high_yield_pearls: Central obesity, moon facies, buffalo hump, purple striae, thin skin, hyperglycemia, HTN, hypokalemic alkalosis = classic Cushing.
Most common overall cause = exogenous glucocorticoids. Most common endogenous cause = Cushing disease (pituitary ACTH adenoma).
Low-dose dexamethasone suppression test screens for Cushing; high-dose test distinguishes pituitary (suppresses) from ectopic ACTH (does not suppress) and adrenal sources (ACTH suppressed).
Ectopic ACTH classically from small cell lung cancer — patients often have severe hypokalemia, metabolic alkalosis, hyperpigmentation, and little time for classic body habitus to develop.
Cortisol spillover to mineralocorticoid receptors causes hypertension + hypokalemia + metabolic alkalosis.
sources: First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Endocrine chapter, 'Cushing syndrome' section.
